teachSlides.title: What is an index, like the S&P 500, in plain English?
teachSlides.body:
A scoreboard that tracks a big group of companies. The S&P 500 follows roughly 500 large U.S. companies — not one stock, a whole basket.

**Example:** Instead of grading one student, you average the grades of 500 students to see how “the whole grade” is doing.

teachSlides.title: What is an ETF (simple version)?
teachSlides.body:
A fund you can buy and sell like a stock, which usually holds many companies or bonds inside one ticker — a handy way to diversify.

**Example:** A smoothie: many fruits blended together. One cup, many ingredients.

teachSlides.title: What is diversification?
teachSlides.body:
Not putting all your money in one company or one bet. If one part struggles, other parts can balance the ride.

**Example:** A sports team: one injured player hurts less if the rest of the roster is strong.

teachSlides.title: Why does starting earlier often matter more than the exact stock you pick?
teachSlides.body:
Because time lets compounding (growth on growth) work. Starting late means you miss years of potential snowballing.

**Example:** Two snowballs rolling downhill: the one that starts higher on the hill ends much bigger, even with the same snow.

teachSlides.title: What is compound growth in one picture?
teachSlides.body:
Your money earns returns, and then those returns can earn returns too — like a snowball picking up more snow as it rolls.

**Example:** $100 grows 8% to $108. Next year 8% applies to $108, not just the original $100.

teachSlides.title: Why do many teachers say “investing is emotional,” not just math?
teachSlides.body:
Because fear and greed make people sell low after drops or chase hype at the top. Staying calm is a skill.

**Example:** Panic-selling after a bad quiz can make you quit school; zooming out helps you recover and learn.

teachSlides.title: What is a simple way to think about risk?
teachSlides.body:
The chance you lose money or sleep badly because prices swing. More possible reward often comes with more possible pain.

**Example:** Roller coasters: bigger drops, bigger thrills — not everyone wants that ride with lunch money.

teachSlides.title: What does it mean to “do your own research” (DYOR)?
teachSlides.body:
Read what the company does, how it makes money, and what could go wrong — instead of trusting a random caption online.

**Example:** Before adopting a pet, you read about food, space, and vet costs — same idea for a stock.

teachSlides.title: Why is a brokerage account not the same as a bank account?
teachSlides.body:
A brokerage is where you place orders to buy/sell investments. Money and investments there face market risk — it is not the same as FDIC-insured cash rules (varies by country and product).

**Example:** Bank vault for cash vs. workshop where you build a bookshelf — different tools, different risks.

recapSlide.title: ⚡ Level 2 — Building blocks
recapSlide.body:
Funds, diversification, habits, and why emotions matter as much as numbers.

Start with the first card anytime you need a reset.
